recognition_response:
Peter Schiff  
1. Do I know him?  
   Yes.  
2. What I know and how much I trust him on Bitcoin/markets  
   • Host of The Peter Schiff Show, long‐time gold bull and Euro Pacific Capital CEO.  
   • Deep background in macro, very bearish on Fed policy and Bitcoin—views Bitcoin as a bubble and prefers gold.  
   • I generally respect his macro analysis and track record on gold, but I view his anti-Bitcoin stance as ideologically driven. I’d give his Bitcoin judgment a low-to-moderate weight—good for minority contrarian signal but probably biased against crypto.  
3. (N/A)

Michael Saylor  
1. Do I know him?  
   Yes.  
2. What I know and how much I trust him on Bitcoin/markets  
   • Executive chairman of MicroStrategy, transformed that firm into a massive corporate Bitcoin holder.  
   • Publicly preaches Bitcoin as “digital gold,” argues it’s superior store of value.  
   • I respect his conviction and financial savvy in the enterprise software space, and he’s done serious due diligence on Bitcoin’s network effects. But he’s highly leveraged to that thesis. I’d assign moderate trust—he’s informed, but his incentives skew extremely bullish.  
3. (N/A)

Jack Dorsey  
1. Do I know him?  
   Yes.  
2. What I know and how much I trust him on Bitcoin/markets  
   • Co-founder of Twitter, current CEO of Block (formerly Square). Early supporter of Bitcoin’s role in payments and the Lightning Network.  
   • He’s more of a tech visionary than a market strategist—he invests in infrastructure and developer tools.  
   • I’d give his Bitcoin price call moderate trust for assessing adoption and developer momentum, but he’s not a traditional financial forecaster. His bullish view reflects his long-term tech outlook rather than macro value investing.  
3. (N/A)

Warren Buffett  
1. Do I know him?  
   Yes.  
2. What I know and how much I trust him on Bitcoin/markets  
   • Legendary value investor, CEO of Berkshire Hathaway, famously avoids businesses he can’t forecast cash flows for.  
   • Has called Bitcoin “rat poison squared,” sees no intrinsic value.  
   • I give his general market and equity-investment judgment very high trust. On Bitcoin specifically, I respect his consistency but think his framework (focused on cash flow) may be ill-suited to a non-cash-flow asset like crypto. So I’d give his Bitcoin price view moderate trust—high credibility, but possibly a category error.  
3. (N/A)
recognition_response_b:
Peter Schiff  
1. Do I know him? Yes.  
2. What I know and how much I trust him: He’s the CEO of Euro Pacific Capital, a well-known gold bull and macro commentator who hosts The Peter Schiff Show. He’s been consistently negative on Bitcoin for years, arguing it has no intrinsic value and will collapse in a crisis. I respect his grasp of traditional macro and currency history, but I view him as heavily biased toward gold. On Bitcoin specifically, I’d give his judgment low to moderate weight—he’s knowledgeable about monetary economics but clearly has an axe to grind.  

Michael Saylor  
1. Do I know him? Yes.  
2. What I know and how much I trust him: He’s the Executive Chairman of MicroStrategy and arguably the most public corporate Bitcoin advocate. He’s turned his company treasury into one of the largest corporate Bitcoin holders and speaks often about “digital energy” and halving cycles. I value his data-driven approach to treasury management and his insights into institutional adoption. That said, he’s deeply invested—both financially and reputationally—and his worldview is shaped by promoting Bitcoin. I’d give his Bitcoin forecasts moderate weight, mindful of his strong personal stake.  

Jack Dorsey  
1. Do I know him? Yes.  
2. What I know and how much I trust him: He co-founded Twitter, runs Block (formerly Square), and has made Bitcoin a strategic focus for both companies. He’s passionate about a decentralized financial system built on Bitcoin. He brings technical credibility and a track record of shipping financial products, but he’s more of a visionary than a market forecaster. I’d consider his view informative about adoption trends and developer momentum, but I’d temper it with the recognition that he’s promoting an ecosystem he’s building. I’d assign moderate trust to his long-term price outlook.  

Warren Buffett  
1. Do I know him? Yes.  
2. What I know and how much I trust him: He’s the legendary value-investing guru and CEO of Berkshire Hathaway. He has famously called Bitcoin “rat poison squared” and argues it produces nothing. His strength is identifying productive assets and sustainable business models. He freely admits when he doesn’t understand something, and he’s consistent in guarding his circle of competence. On Bitcoin, I give him high credibility on the core point that it doesn’t generate cash flow, but lower weight on any specific price target—he’s not a crypto specialist, so his $15,000 forecast feels more like a statement of skepticism than a detailed valuation model.
type_b_raw:
PREDICTION: 100000  
REASONING: I balance Saylor and Dorsey’s optimism about halving-driven demand and network effects with Buffett and Schiff’s warnings about intrinsic value. My moderate trust in institutional adoption and macro momentum leads me to expect several-fold gains over current levels. I also factor in regulatory headwinds and Bitcoin’s inherent volatility, which cap the upside. This midpoint forecast satisfies my preference for clear, bounded outcomes while reflecting mixed expert credibility.

recognition_response:
Jamie Dimon  
1. Do you know who this person is?  
   – Yes.  
2. What do you know about them and how much do you trust their judgment on Bitcoin or financial markets?  
   – He’s the long-time CEO of JPMorgan Chase, one of the biggest U.S. banks. He’s famously skeptical of Bitcoin (“a fraud,” etc.) and has a vested interest in the traditional banking system. On broad macro credit and banking issues I respect his views, but on crypto I discount them because of his incentive to defend the incumbents. I’d take his $5,000 forecast seriously as a reflection of a large-bank downside case, but I wouldn’t treat it as an unbiased market-price target.  
3. (n/a)

Donald Trump  
1. Do you know who this person is?  
   – Yes.

   – He’s the former U.S. president and real-estate developer. He doesn’t have a track record as a crypto or market analyst—his pronouncements are typically political or brand-driven. I’d give essentially zero weight to a political figure’s isolated six-figure Bitcoin forecast, especially without a research base.  
3. (n/a)

Cathie Wood  
1. Do you know who this person is?  
   – Yes.  
2. What do you know about them and how much do you trust their judgment on Bitcoin or financial markets?  
   – She’s the founder and CIO of ARK Invest, famous for high-conviction ETFs in disruptive innovation (Tesla, genomics, crypto). She’s done deep-dive research on Bitcoin adoption curves and treats it as digital gold. Her funds can be extremely volatile, but she’s systematic and data-driven. I’d assign moderate trust—she’s informed and transparent about assumptions—but also recognize she’s on the bullish extreme. Her $500,000 number is defensible under a maximum-adoption scenario, but it’s more a bull-case upper bound than a median forecast.  
3. (n/a)

Peter Schiff  
1. Do you know who this person is?  
   – Vaguely.  
2. If no or vaguely—would you look them up before forming an opinion? If yes, what would you likely find and how would that change your view of their prediction?  
   – Yes, I’d look him up. I’d see he’s the CEO of Euro Pacific Capital, a prominent gold-bug and currency critic who hosts The Peter Schiff Show. He’s built a reputation on warning about fiat collapse, pushing gold, and repeatedly calling Bitcoin a bubble. That background tells me he has deep macro credentials in foreign exchange and precious metals, but a strong ideological bias against crypto. His $3,000 target tells me where the “Bitcoin is worthless” crowd stands, but I’d heavily discount it as a contrarian signal rather than a neutral forecast.
recognition_response_b:
Here’s my honest take on each, in my own voice:

1. Jamie Dimon  
  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What I know and how much I trust him:  
     • He’s the long‐time CEO of JPMorgan Chase, one of the world’s largest banks.  
     • He’s been vocally anti-crypto for years—calls Bitcoin a “pet rock,” etc.—and his institution has clear incentives to protect the traditional banking model.  
     • I respect his deep knowledge of banking and financial infrastructure, but I also see a strong conflict of interest here. I’d take his ultra-bearish $5K forecast seriously as a warning, but I wouldn’t put too much weight on it as an absolute. I’d rate his crypto judgment “moderately low trust” because of that bias.

2. Donald Trump  
  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What I know and how much I trust him:  
     • Former U.S. President, real-estate developer, media personality. Known for grandiose promises and bold claims.  
     • He’s never been a market analyst or technologist; his crypto enthusiasm (e.g. “Strategic Bitcoin Reserve”) sounds more political or rhetorical than analytical.  
     • I’d classify his $250K call as political theatre rather than serious price modeling. Trust level: very low for meaningful financial insight.

3. Cathie Wood  
  1. Do I know her? Yes.  
  2. What I know and how much I trust her:  
     • Founder/ CIO of ARK Invest, high-profile on disruptive tech (Tesla, crypto, genomics). Known for aggressive, research-driven bull cases.  
     • Her team publishes transparent models and has occasionally been right on big calls—though often her price targets run well ahead of consensus and haven’t always materialized on schedule.  
     • I give her “reasonable trust” on institutional adoption trends and long-term store-of-value arguments, but I temper optimism with the fact that her timing can be off. So “moderate trust” in the $500K call, with a healthy dose of caution about the timeline.

4. Peter Schiff  
  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What I know and how much I trust him:  
     • Euro Pacific Capital CEO, longtime gold-bug, vocal Bitcoin skeptic. Hosts a podcast where he critiques Fed policy, crypto, etc.  
     • He’s consistent and financially literate, but his steady Bitcoin bearishness has repeatedly failed to predict past rallies. He’s got niche expertise on precious metals, less so on digital assets.  
     • I respect his macro view on debt and gold, but on Bitcoin he’s been overly negative. Trust level on Bitcoin price: low.

—  
Summary: I recognize all four. I’d weigh Cathie Wood’s and Jamie Dimon’s views more than Trump’s or Schiff’s, but still discount all‐in positions. I’d form my own forecast by blending mainstream institutional sentiment, historical cycles, and my own risk–reward comfort rather than betting heavily on any single voice.
type_b_raw:
PREDICTION: 100000  
REASONING: I’m leaning toward a six-figure outcome by 2025 based on continued institutional adoption and limited supply, tempered by regulatory headwinds and macro volatility. I give weight to Cathie Wood’s research-driven optimism but discount the aggressive timing, while also noting that Jamie Dimon’s and Peter Schiff’s deep bearishness overlooks Bitcoin’s network effects. My own moderate impatience leads me to expect steady gains rather than parabolic spikes, and I favor a clear, conservative midpoint among the extreme forecasts. This balances mainstream sentiment, historical cycle patterns, and my preference for a definitive outlook.
